executive_summary: On October 6, 2024, an attacker executed a price-manipulation attack targeting the SASHA token on Ethereum, exploiting thin liquidity conditions to extract approximately 249 ETH (~$600,000 USD). The attack was carried out through a single transaction and leveraged vulnerabilities arising from the token's shallow liquidity pools.
attack_narrative: The attacker (0x991493900674b10bdf54bdfe95b4e043257798cf) initiated the exploit via transaction 0xd9fdc7d03eec28fc2453c5fa68eff82d4c297f436a6a5470c54ca3aecd2db17e. The attack exploited extremely thin liquidity in SASHA token pools on Ethereum. By manipulating prices across low-liquidity pairs—likely using flash loans or large capital movements through Uniswap V2 (0x7a250d5630B4cF539739dF2C5dAcb4c659F2488D) and possibly Uniswap V3 (0x68b3465833fb72A70ecDF485E0e4C7bD8665Fc45)—the attacker was able to artificially inflate or deflate SASHA's price to extract value from protocols relying on its on-chain price feed. The involvement of WETH (0xC02aaA39b223FE8D0A0e5C4F27eAD9083C756Cc2) confirms the attacker converted profits to native wrapped Ether. A proof-of-concept has been published by DeFiHackLabs, confirming the attack vector involved SASHA token price manipulation.
attacker_motive: The attacker's primary motive was financial profit through exploitation of a low-liquidity token whose price could be easily manipulated. By targeting thin SASHA liquidity, the attacker could profit from price discrepancies induced across DeFi integrations that used the manipulated token as a price oracle or collateral asset, ultimately extracting 249 ETH.
key_takeaway: This incident underscores the systemic risk posed by protocols integrating low-liquidity tokens as price sources or collateral. Without robust oracle protections—such as TWAPs, liquidity thresholds, or manipulation-resistant feeds—thinly traded tokens remain vulnerable to price manipulation attacks. DeFi protocols should enforce minimum liquidity requirements and implement circuit breakers when detecting abnormal price movements in dependent assets.
